name: Furies Extended
description: The Furies, also known as the Erinyes or Eumenides, are chthonic goddesses of vengeance in Greek mythology. They are typically depicted as female figures with snakes for hair, dog heads, bat wings, and bloodshot eyes. Their primary function is to pursue and punish those who have committed heinous crimes, particularly those against family members, oaths, or suppliants. They are relentless and implacable, driving their victims mad with guilt and torment. While initially viewed as terrifying and vengeful spirits, the Furies evolved in later myths to represent a more complex concept of justice and moral order. They are associated with curses, madness, and the underworld, embodying the consequences of wrongdoing. The Furies are not merely instruments of punishment but also guardians of ancient laws and moral codes, ensuring that transgressions do not go unpunished. They are a powerful representation of the psychological and spiritual repercussions of guilt and the inescapable nature of justice. Their presence serves as a constant reminder of the importance of upholding moral principles and the consequences of violating sacred bonds. They are often depicted as a trio, though their number varies in different accounts, each embodying a specific aspect of vengeance and retribution. They are formidable adversaries, capable of driving even the strongest individuals to despair and madness. Over time, they transitioned from terrifying pursuers to more benevolent protectors of justice, reflecting a shift in the Greek understanding of morality and punishment.
subtitle: Goddesses of Vengeance and Retribution
appearance: The Furies are often depicted as hideous, winged women with snakes for hair. They have bloodshot eyes, dog heads, and carry whips or torches. Their clothing is ragged and stained with blood, reflecting their association with violence and retribution. They are fearsome and terrifying in appearance, inspiring dread in those who behold them.
habitat: The Furies reside in the Underworld, often near the throne of Hades. They also roam the earth, pursuing those who have incurred their wrath. They are associated with dark and desolate places, reflecting their role as agents of punishment and vengeance.
behavior: The Furies are driven by a relentless desire for vengeance. They are implacable and unforgiving, pursuing their victims relentlessly until they are driven to madness or death. They are fiercely protective of familial bonds and oaths, punishing those who violate these sacred principles. While initially terrifying, some myths depict them as evolving to represent a more balanced form of justice.

keyMyths.title: Orestes' Trial
keyMyths.description: Orestes is pursued by the Furies for the matricide of his mother, Clytemnestra. He seeks refuge in Athens, where Athena establishes a court to try him, ultimately acquitting him and transforming the Furies into the Eumenides (the Kindly Ones).

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: A Greek tragedy that explores the transformation of the Furies into the Eumenides.
sources.text: A Greek tragedy that explores the transformation of the Furies into the Eumenides.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Describes the birth of the Furies from the blood of Uranus.
